coredata.dc:title: A molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor for sensitive detection of tetracycline using nitrogen-doped carbon dots-embedded zinc-based metal-organic frameworks as signal-amplifying tags
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jinni
Li, Yan
Teng, Longhao
Cao, Yichuan
Hu, Xuelian
Fang, Guozhen
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline (TC) residues not only endanger human health, but also are detrimental to the sustainable development of aquaculture and animal husbandry. Herein, a novel fluorescence sensor with high sensitivity and selectivity was developed based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots embedded in zinc-based metal-organic frameworks and incorporating molecularly imprinted polymer (ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP). The physical and chemical properties of the ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP had been characterized by SEM, TEM, FTIR, XRD, BET, TGA, etc. Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection (LOD) of the novel sensor was 0.045 μg mL−1 with the concentration of TC in the range of 0.1–4.0 μg mL−1. In addition, the prepared imprinted polymers showed superior adsorption selectivity to tetracycline compared with non-imprinted polymers, and the quenching mechanism of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP was demonstrated to be attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE). This work provided an effective and reliable method for the specific detection of tetracycline and was successfully applied in milk and egg samples with satisfactory recoveries (80.67–95.22%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Tetracycline
Fluorescence sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst misctext primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of actual samples by HPLC

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Tianjin University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Come to the conclusion, a novel metal-organic framework-supported molecularly imprinted nitrogen-doped carbon dots sensor was fabricated through a simple sol-gel method, which integrated the merits of ZIF-8, N-CDs and MIT. Therefore, the fluorescence sensor demonstrated the attractive advantages of approving sensitivity and high selectivity in the determination of TC, with a wide concentration (0.1–4.0 μg mL−1) and a low LOD (0.045 μg mL−1). More importantly, ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP based sensor was employed to the detection of TC in milk and egg samples with well-pleasing recovery (80.67%–95.22%), revealing great practical application potential. Furthermore, the proposed method supplied a novel and efficient method for a wide range of analytes, implying that the developed quantification method could have more widespread application prospects.This work was supported by the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science & Technology (No. SKLFNS-KF-202208).
This work was supported by the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety , Tianjin University of Science & Technology (No. SKLFNS-KF-202208 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of N-CDs (a) and ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP (b).
SEM images of ZIF-8&N-CDs (a) ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP (b) and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP (c), TEM images of ZIF-8&N-CDs (d) ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP (e) and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP (f).

The FT-IR spectrums of ZIF-8, ZIF-8&N-CDs, ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP (a), powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns of ZIF-8, ZIF-8&N-CDs, ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP (b), nitrogen adsorption and desorption curves of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP (c), TGA curves of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP (d).

Fluorescence emission spectra of ZIF-8&N-CDs, ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP before and after adsorbing TC (a), UV–vis absorption spectra of TC, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP (b), fluorescence emission decay curves of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP alone and ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP with 4 μg mL−1 concentrations of TC under excitation at 360 nm (c), effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP alone and ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP with 4 μg mL−1 concentrations of TC (d), fluorescence response of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP at different contact times (e), effect of pH values (4–10) on the fluorescence intensity of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP alone and ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP with 4 μg mL−1 concentrations of TC (f).
Fluorescence intensity of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP (a) and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP (b) at different concentrations of TC (the linear relationship was shown in (c) and (d), respectively).

The comparison between fluorescence responses of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP to TC and the responses to the other analogues (a), the effect of OTC, CTC and CAP (20 μg mL−1) on the binding of TC (4 μg mL−1) to ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP (b), influence of coexisting interferences (20 μg mL−1) on the fluorescence queuing efficiency of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP in the presence of TC (4 μg mL−1) (c), the effect of irradiation time at 360 nm and storage time on the fluorescence intensity of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP (d).
Determination of TC in real samples by the proposed ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and HPLC.

head.ce:title: A molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor for sensitive detection of tetracycline using nitrogen-doped carbon dots-embedded zinc-based metal-organic frameworks as signal-amplifying tags

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin, 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding, 071001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding 071001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracycline (TC) residues not only endanger human health, but also are detrimental to the sustainable development of aquaculture and animal husbandry. Herein, a novel fluorescence sensor with high sensitivity and selectivity was developed based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots embedded in zinc-based metal-organic frameworks and incorporating molecularly imprinted polymer (ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP). The physical and chemical properties of the ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP had been characterized by SEM, TEM, FTIR, XRD, BET, TGA, etc. Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection (LOD) of the novel sensor was 0.045 μg mL−1 with the concentration of TC in the range of 0.1–4.0 μg mL−1. In addition, the prepared imprinted polymers showed superior adsorption selectivity to tetracycline compared with non-imprinted polymers, and the quenching mechanism of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP was demonstrated to be attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE). This work provided an effective and reliable method for the specific detection of tetracycline and was successfully applied in milk and egg samples with satisfactory recoveries (80.67–95.22%).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is a commonly used antibiotic drug, which was used mainly in humans and animals to treat bacterial infections [1]. However, the overuse of tetracycline leads to excessive residues in animal foodstuffs, like meat, egg and milk. TC residues can enter the human body mainly via the food chain, and maybe harmful to health, mainly in the aspects of intestinal flora imbalance, damage to the human immune system, allergic reactions, and pathological changes in body organs [2]. According to the national food safety standard (GB 31650-2019), the maximum residue limit in milk and egg is 100 and 400 μg kg−1, respectively. Consequently, the existing detection methods for TC have attracted the attention of researchers worldwide and will build on their solid work. So far, the existing detection methods include microbiology [3], liquid chromatography [4], immunoassay [5,6], capillary electrophoresis [7] and sensor method [8]. Although, above methods have the advantages of excellent recovery rate and high accuracy, they also exist shortcomings of complex pretreatment process, time-consuming and high costs and complex equipment operations. In comparison, fluorescence sensing technology is fast, accurate, simple, low-cost and easy to operate, so it is gradually becoming an extensive research topic in the detection of TC.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon nano-fluorescent material with a three-dimensional size below 10 nm [9–11]. Not only the excellent optical properties similar to quantum dots, CDs also have the advantages of wide excitation spectrum, good dispersion, hydrophilicity, biocompatibility and low toxicity [12–14], Thus, CDs have been widely investigated and shown broad prospects in the fields of photocatalysis [15,16] nanosensors [17–19], fluorescent marker [20], bioimaging, drug delivery, antibacterial and cancer therapy [21–23]. However, some of the CDs often suffer from serious fluorescence aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [24], which limited further application. An effective strategy for preventing the quenching effect is by dispersing nanoparticles within a host matrix.

Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) are crystal skeletons formed by metal nodes or metal clusters bridged by organic ligands [25–27]. Compared to traditional inorganic materials and porous carbon materials, MOFs have the following advantages: 1) a single crystal structure, which can clearly explain its structural characteristics; 2) large porosity and specific surface area; 3) the structure of MOF has diversity and tunability; 4) It has certain flexibility; 5) MOFs are easy to be modified or functionalized [28]. These advantages make MOFs have potential applications in many fields, like gas adsorption and separation, catalysis, proton conductors, luminescence and sensing [29,30]. Zeolitic imidazolate framework compounds (ZIFs), as a classic type of MOFs materials, mainly utilize organic imidazolate ligands and metal ions to self-assemble through coordination to form zeolite-like (organic zeolite analogues) compounds with supramolecular microporous network structure [31]. Among many ZIFs materials, ZIF-8 has attracted much attention because of its highly desirable properties like ease of synthesis, water solubility [32,33] and outstanding stability [34]. The porous nature of ZIF-8 can be used as a host matrix to embed CDs, not only avoiding fluorescence aggregation-caused quenching of CDs, but also enhancing the efficient accumulation of analyte. However, the proposed methods were applied to detect a class of analytes, and lacked of specificity for a specific analyte. Hence, it was an urgent necessity to explore a new strategy to increase the specificity of constructed fluorescence sensors.

Molecular imprinting technology (MIT) is a preparative technique based on the specific binding principle of antibody and antigen [35,36]. The synthesized three-dimensional cross-linked polymers with specific recognition site are called molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs). MIPs had many preparative techniques, like pre-determined molecular recognition capability, simple preparation, good stability [37]. Thus, MIPs have attracted the attention of researchers and have been widely used in many fields, including solid phase extraction [38], chromatographic resolution [39] and chemical sensors [40] and so on.

Inspired by these results, an innovative fluorescent sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots embedded in zinc-based metal-organic frameworks and incorporating molecularly imprinted polymer was constructed. In this experiment, nitrogen-doped carbon dots with excellent fluorescent properties were used as sensing signals, and porous ZIF-8 with large specific surface areas were used as supporting carriers to improve the adsorption capacity of the composite system. Metal-organic framework-supported molecularly imprinted carbon dots (ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP) was prepared via a simple sol-gel strategy. The specific synthesis process was shown in Fig. 1 . In the polymerization process, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were employed as the functional monomer and cross-linking agent, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescence sensor responded well to TC (0.1–4.0 μg mL−1), and with a low LOD of 0.045 μg mL−1. What's more, the proposed strategy was successfully used for the sensitive and accurate detection of TC in actual samples (milk and egg).

3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), tetracycline (TC) were purchased from TCI Development Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 2-methylimidazole (2-Hmin) were obtained from Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, KCl, FeCl3, l-Histidine (L-His), l-Tyrosine (L-Tyr), l-Cysteine (L-Cys), L-Tryptophan (L-Trp), l-Phenylalanine (L-Phe), glucose and sucrose were supplied by Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used throughout the experimental procedures. All chemical reagents were analytical grade without any purification.

Absorbance spectra were recorded by a Evolution 200 UV–vis spectrophotometer (USA). Fluorescence measurements were measured on a Hitachi F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were tested at ambient temperature on a Tensor-37 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany). The size and structure of the polymers were collected with a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, talos G2 200X, USA) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Apreo, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were analyzed by Siemens D5000 X-ray diffractometer (Germany). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was recorded by thermal gravity analysis (Q50, USA).
According to the hydrothermal method previously reported with some modifications [41]. The specific synthesis method in the Supplementary information.

ZIF-8&N-CDs were prepared referring to the method previously reported with slight change [42]. Typically, 0.98 mmol Zn(NO3)2·6H2O was dispersed in 20 mL of methanol, then 200 μL N-CDs was mixed with the above solution. After stirring for 0.5 h, 20 mL of methanol solution containing 7.90 mmol of 2-methylimidazole was added and the mixture continued to stir for 1 h. The resulting product was washed three times with methanol to remove unreacted residues. Finally, the reactants were collected by drying in a vacuum oven at 55 °C.

The ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP was prepared referring to the method reported previously with slight change [43]. First, 30 mg of ZIF-8&N-CDs were dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol, assisted by ultrasonication for 0.5 h. Then, 0.1 mmol of TC and 0.6 mmol of APTES were dripped and the pre-polymerization solution was stood for 1 h at ambient temperatures. Subsequently, 2 mmol of TEOS and 1 mL of HOAc were sequentially added the above solution and stirred for 12 h. To get rid of the template molecule, the resulting polymers were collected via ultrasound-assisted centrifugation and washed several times with methanol until no template can be detected from the eluates by UV–vis spectroscopy. Finally, the reactants were obtained by vacuum drying at 50 °C for overnight. NIP was synthesized in the same method, but without TC added.

In this experiment, F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to analyse the prepared materials. Both excitation and emission slits were 10 nm. Then 1 mg of the materials was dispersed in 2 mL of PBS solution evenly by ultrasound and oscillated for 30 min at ambient temperatures. Finally, the solution was transferred to a cuvette to measure the fluorescence intensity. Each experiment was repeated for three times.

In order to evaluate the practical application of the proposed method for the detection of trace TC, two different samples of milk and egg were purchased from a local supermarket. The preprocessor was designed according to the literature with minor modifications [44]. The detailed procedure was in the Supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of actual samples by HPLC
ce:section.ce:para: The pretreatment process of the samples (milk and egg) and the detection conditions referred to the National Standards of the People's Republic of China (GB/T 22990-2008). The determination was performed by HPLC system with an ultraviolet detector. The separation was carried out with a reversed phase column (C18, 250 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 μm particle size) with a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1 and column temperature of 40 °C. The injection volume was 20 μL, and the detection wavelength was 350 nm.

To attain ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP featured with the well-pleasing specificity and sensitivity for the target analyte, the corresponding synthesis conditions, involving the molar ratio of TC to APTES to TEOS, addition amount of ZIF-8&N-CDs and the types of solvent were optimized using the imprinting factor (IF) as a measurement index (Tables S1–S3) [45]. The size and structure of the synthesized materials were investigated through SEM and TEM images.Fig. S1a and Fig. 2a exhibited that both ZIF-8 and ZIF-8&N-CDs had similar morphology of rhombic dodecahedron characteristic structure with the uniform size about 180 nm. Compared to ZIF-8 and ZIF-8&N-CDs, the surfaces of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP (Fig. 2 b and c) were relatively rough, probably because the surface of ZIF-8&N-CDs was modified with an imprinted layer and the uniform size was about 250 nm. Meanwhile, the results of TEM (Fig. S1b and Fig. 2d-f) were consistent with the SEM results. The above phenomenon preliminarily proved that the ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP was successfully synthesized.

The FT-IR spectra of synthesized materials were also measured. As presented in Fig. S2, the peaks at 3062 and 1565 cm−1 were respectively corresponding to the stretching vibration of O–H and CO, which were the functional groups on N-CDs surfaces. With regard to ZIF-8 (Fig. 3 a), the peaks at 1600 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of CN in the imidazole ring. The typical peak at 422 cm−1 was determined to be the stretching vibration of the Zn–N bond. There were stretching bands of C–H at around 2940 cm−1, 1312 cm−1 and 1430 cm−1, and the band at 3420 cm−1 belong to N–H. In addition, the strong and broad peak around 1056 cm−1 appeared due to the Si–O–Si asymmetric stretching, while the vibration of Si–O can also be observed at around 769 cm−1 and 457 cm−1. These functional groups were from APTES and TEOS, which indicated that the imprinting layer was bound to the ZIF-8&N-CDs, resoundingly.

The crystal structures of the synthesized materials were analyzed and the XRD patterns were exhibited in Fig. 3b. Obviously, the diffraction peaks of bare ZIF-8 were consistent with the simulated data of the literature [46]. After embedding the N-CDs into ZIF-8, the intensities of the diffraction peaks were significantly enhanced, indicating a higher crystallization degree, which illustrated that ZIF-8&N-CDs were synthesized [47]. However, it was particularly noted that the characteristic diffraction peaks of ZIF-8 largely disappeared after being decorated with imprinting layer, which further indicated that the imprinting layer was successfully modified [48].

The porosity of synthesized materials was proved by a nitrogen adsorption experiment. The surface areas of ZIF-8, ZIF-8&N-CDs (Fig. S3), ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP (Fig. 3c) were 1591, 1561, 15 and 8 m2 g−1, respectively. Apparently, the surface area of ZIF-8&N-CDs slightly decreased, in comparison to ZIF-8, which suggested N-CDs were resoundingly capsulized into ZIF-8 to construct hybrid material ZIF-8&N-CDs [49]. Furthermore, the surface area of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP remarkably decreased, mainly due to the decoration of the imprinting layers. To sum up, those results clarified that a core-shell sensor was successfully prepared through N-CDs embedded ZIF-8 coupled with molecularly imprinted polymer.

The TGA analysis was performed to evaluate the thermostability of the synthetic materials. As shown in Fig. 3d, from 25 °C to 110 °C, the weight loss of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP was approximately 6.50% due to the volatilization of the surface water and the decomposition of the incompletely eluted target. For ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP, the weight loss was only due to the evaporation of moisture from the surface of the material. Because of the decomposition of the molecularly imprinted layer and the non-imprinted layer, the weight loss of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP was 21.80% and 17.45% at 110–393 °C, respectively. Furthermore, weight loss at 393–700 °C was due to the decomposition of the metal organic framework, similar decreasing trends for ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP demonstrated that the imprinted layer was wrapped on ZIF-8&N-CDs.

The optical properties of the above sensors were investigated by fluorescence spectra. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence spectrum of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and NIP was consistent with that of ZIF-8&N-CDs, revealing that the decoration of the imprinting layer hardly influenced the luminescence performance of ZIF-8&N-CDs. ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP/NIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs exhibited the same emission peaks at 440 nm under 360 nm excitation. Moreover, the fluorescence response of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP/NIP to TC molecules was explored, and it was found that the fluorescence of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP was effectively quenched in the presence of TC. However, compared with ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP, the response of ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP to TC was weak, which was because there were binding sites on the ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP to TC.

As seen in Fig. 4b, the absorption spectrum of TC partially overlaps with the excitation spectrum of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP, and little overlaps between the emission spectra of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP, which preliminarily indicated the existence of inner filter effect (IFE) instead of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the sensor and the target. In addition, in order to further verify the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of the sensing system was investigated in the presence and absence of the target. As shown in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence lifetime of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP was basically unchanged after the adsorption of TC, further demonstrating that the mechanism that led to the quenching of the fluorescence was mainly due to IFE rather than FRET [50,51].

The concentration of the probe was optimized, to enhance the analytical performance of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP first increased and then decreased with the probe concentration changed from 0.05 to 2 mg mL−1, reaching the maximum value at 0.5 mg mL−1, and a similar trend was observed after adding TC. Therefore, the probe concentration was set to 0.5 mg mL−1 for subsequent experiments. Besides, the effect of temperature in the detection process was studied. As presented in Fig. 4d, the prepared fluorescence probe maintained a stable fluorescence intensity even if the temperature rose to 65 °C, indicating that temperature had a negligible effect on ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP. After the introduction of TC, the fuorescence response of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP to TC in the reaction system hardly changed, illustrating that the temperature had no significant impact on the detection of TC. As can be seen from Fig. 4e, the fluorescence response of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP/NIP to TC showed a rapid increase in 30 min and then almost remained unchanged. Apparently, compared to ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP, ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP had higher fluorescence response ability to TC, which was mainly due to the specific binding site to TC on ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP, but no binding site on ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP. Additionally, 30 min was confirmed as the optimal adsorption equilibrium time. In addition, the pH of the sensor system was optimized. As shown in Fig. 4f, with the change of pH from 4 to 10, the fluorescence intensity of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP first increased and then decreased, and when the pH value was 7, the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum, and the binding ability of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP to TC was also the strongest. Therefore, pH = 7 was the optimal condition and used for the following experiment.

The fluorescence response of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP to TC was analyzed. As depicted in Fig. 5 a and b, both the fluorescence of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP was quenched with an increase of TC concentration (0.1–4.0 μg mL−1). However, the degree of quenching of ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP was much lower than that of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP. Meanwhile, the fluorescence quenching of the materials toward TC can be analyzed via the Stern-Volmer equation.
F 0
/ F = K s v
C
T C
+ 1
Where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP suspension solution without and with added TC, respectively; Ksv is the quenching constant and C
TC represents the concentration of TC. The relevant linear equations of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP were
F 0
/ F = 0.1009
C
T C
+ 1.0075
(Fig. 5c) and
F 0
/ F = 0.0381
C
T C
+ 0.9968
(Fig. 5d), with a linear correlation coefficient R
2 of 0.998 and 0.996, respectively. The linear range of the sensor was 0.1–4.0 μg mL−1 with the LOD of 0.045 μg mL−1. The above values were obtained according to 3σ criteria (3σ/S, σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution, and S is the slope of the linear equation). The comprehensive comparison of the fluorescence response value (F
0
/F) and the slope of linear equation between ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP and ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP showed that ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP had excellent recognition ability and high selectivity for TC.

To investigate the selectivity of the fluorescence sensor to TC and its structural analogues, three structural analogues of TC (chloroamphenicol (CAP) oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC) (Fig. S5) were selected as structural and functional competing substances of TC. From Fig. 6 a, ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP showed higher selectivity toward TC than other competing substances, which was mainly due to the specific binding site of TC on the ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP. However, the selectivity of ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP to TC was less obvious, due to the fact that there were no specific binding sites in ZIF-8&N-CDs@NIP. Besides, competitive adsorption experiments were also carried out. As described in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence response of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP to TC was not affected even in the presence of competing substances. In order to further investigate the anti-interference ability of the ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP sensor in complex environments, common ions in food such as: Ions (Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Fe3+, Cl− and NO3 −), amino acids (L-His, L-Tyr, L-Cys, L-Trp and L-Phe), and sugars (glucose and sucrose) were chosen as interfering substrate. As shown in Fig. 6c, these substances had no significant effect on the sensor even at 5 times the mass concentration. To sum up, ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP have an obvious ability to specifically detect TC.

At the same time, the photostability of the material was evaluated by continuous ultraviolet irradiation and storage time. From Fig. 6d, the fluorescence intensity of the material did not decrease significantly after the material was continuously irradiated by UV lamp for 60 min. In addition, when the material was stored in a refrigerator at 4°C and the fluorescence intensity was recorded every 10 days, the fluorescence intensity of the material did not change significantly for 60 consecutive days. The results showed that the material had good resistance to photobleaching and storage stability.

To compare the applicability of the proposed method with the traditional determination method (HPLC-UV) to detect TC in actual samples (milk and egg), the spiked recovery experiment was performed by adding standard samples (0, 0.5, 1.5 and 3.0 mg kg−1) in milk and egg. The results were summarized in Table 1 . The recoveries acquired by the proposed method are between 80.67% and 95.22% with RSD of 1.12–4.92%. Evidently, the results of the presented fluorescence method were consistent with those of the HPLC method. As a result, ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP based sensor with good accuracy and reliability has good prospects for application in the determination of TC in food samples. Furthermore, the performance of the developed method was evaluated by comparing it with previous reported approaches for determining TC in Table S4. Evidently, the proposed method in this work performed a lower detection limit, good precision (RSD) and comparable recovery. The high performance of ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP was mainly benefited from two aspects. Firstly, ZIF-8 as support not only increased the surface area, but also avoided the quenching of aggregation of N-CDs, which significantly improved the sensitivity of the sensor. Second, the modification of the molecular imprinting layer improved the selectivity of the sensor. In a word, the developed sensor provided a highly efficient method for trace TC detection in complex systems.

Come to the conclusion, a novel metal-organic framework-supported molecularly imprinted nitrogen-doped carbon dots sensor was fabricated through a simple sol-gel method, which integrated the merits of ZIF-8, N-CDs and MIT. Therefore, the fluorescence sensor demonstrated the attractive advantages of approving sensitivity and high selectivity in the determination of TC, with a wide concentration (0.1–4.0 μg mL−1) and a low LOD (0.045 μg mL−1). More importantly, ZIF-8&N-CDs@MIP based sensor was employed to the detection of TC in milk and egg samples with well-pleasing recovery (80.67%–95.22%), revealing great practical application potential. Furthermore, the proposed method supplied a novel and efficient method for a wide range of analytes, implying that the developed quantification method could have more widespread application prospects.

Jinni Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft, preparation. Yan Li: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Longhao Teng: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Yichuan Cao: Investigation, Validation. Xuelian Hu: Investigation. Guozhen Fang: Supervision. Shuo Wang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science & Technology (No. SKLFNS-KF-202208).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2023.341032.
